series.title: mm/vma: convert vm_flags_t to vma_flags_t in vma code
series.author: "Lorenzo Stoakes (Oracle)" <ljs@kernel.org>

patches.title: [PATCH v2 00/23] mm/vma: convert vm_flags_t to vma_flags_t in vma code
[PATCH v2 01/23] mm/vma: add vma_flags_empty(), vma_flags_and(), vma_flags_diff_pair()
[PATCH v2 02/23] tools/testing/vma: add unit tests flag empty, diff_pair, and[_mask]
[PATCH v2 03/23] mm/vma: add further vma_flags_t unions
[PATCH v2 04/23] tools/testing/vma: convert bulk of test code to vma_flags_t
[PATCH v2 05/23] mm/vma: use new VMA flags for sticky flags logic
[PATCH v2 06/23] tools/testing/vma: fix VMA flag tests
[PATCH v2 07/23] mm/vma: add append_vma_flags() helper
[PATCH v2 08/23] tools/testing/vma: add simple test for append_vma_flags()
[PATCH v2 09/23] mm: unexport vm_brk_flags() and eliminate vm_flags parameter
[PATCH v2 10/23] mm/vma: introduce vma_flags_same[_mask/_pair]()
[PATCH v2 11/23] mm/vma: introduce [vma_flags,legacy]_to_[legacy,vma_flags]() helpers
[PATCH v2 12/23] tools/testing/vma: test that legacy flag helpers work correctly
[PATCH v2 13/23] mm/vma: introduce vma_test[_any[_mask]](), and make inlining consistent
[PATCH v2 14/23] tools/testing/vma: update VMA flag tests to test vma_test[_any_mask]()
[PATCH v2 15/23] mm: introduce vma_flags_count() and vma[_flags]_test_single_mask()
[PATCH v2 16/23] tools/testing/vma: test vma_flags_count,vma[_flags]_test_single_mask
[PATCH v2 17/23] mm: convert do_brk_flags() to use vma_flags_t
[PATCH v2 18/23] mm: update vma_supports_mlock() to use new VMA flags
[PATCH v2 19/23] mm/vma: introduce vma_clear_flags[_mask]()
[PATCH v2 20/23] tools/testing/vma: update VMA tests to test vma_clear_flags[_mask]()
[PATCH v2 21/23] mm/vma: convert as much as we can in mm/vma.c to vma_flags_t
[PATCH v2 22/23] mm/vma: convert vma_modify_flags[_uffd]() to use vma_flags_t
[PATCH v2 23/23] mm/vma: convert __mmap_region() to use vma_flags_t

review.summary: This series converts vm_flags_t to vma_flags_t throughout mm/vma.c and related code, adding bitmap-based helper functions and union aliases for backward compatibility during the transition. The type-system plumbing is sound, but vma_flags_empty() is missing the const qualifier that every other query helper in the same file carries.
review.review_inline:
Lorenzo Stoakes (Oracle) <ljs@kernel.org>

mm/vma: convert vm_flags_t to vma_flags_t in vma code

I cross-referenced the diff against the base mm-new tree.  Verified
the union layout assumptions, sticky-flags semantics, and the
interaction with existing callers.

--- [PATCH v2 01/23] mm/vma: add vma_flags_empty(), vma_flags_and(),
    vma_flags_diff_pair() ---

> diff --git a/include/linux/mm_types.h b/include/linux/mm_types.h
> +/* Are no flags set in the specified VMA flags? */
> +static __always_inline bool vma_flags_empty(vma_flags_t *flags)
> +{
> +	unsigned long *bitmap = flags->__vma_flags;
> +
> +	return bitmap_empty(bitmap, NUM_VMA_FLAG_BITS);
> +}

Every other pure-query function in mm_types.h and mm.h takes a const
pointer: vma_flags_test(), vma_flags_test_any_mask(), and
vma_flags_test_all_mask() all declare "const vma_flags_t *".
vma_flags_empty() only reads the bitmap, so should the signature be:

  bool vma_flags_empty(const vma_flags_t *flags)

with a matching "const unsigned long *bitmap"?  As written, callers
that hold a const-qualified vma_flags_t cannot pass it without a cast.

> diff --git a/include/linux/mm.h b/include/linux/mm.h
> +static __always_inline vma_flags_t vma_flags_and_mask(
> +		const vma_flags_t *flags, vma_flags_t to_and)
> +{
> +	vma_flags_t dst;
> +	unsigned long *bitmap_dst = dst.__vma_flags;
> +	const unsigned long *bitmap = flags->__vma_flags;
> +	const unsigned long *bitmap_to_and = to_and.__vma_flags;
> +
> +	bitmap_and(bitmap_dst, bitmap, bitmap_to_and, NUM_VMA_FLAG_BITS);
> +	return dst;
> +}

Checked: with NUM_VMA_FLAG_BITS == BITS_PER_LONG this reduces to a
single-word AND.  bitmap_and() handles that correctly.
vma_flags_diff_pair() via bitmap_xor() is the same story.  Both look
correct.

[ ... ]

--- [PATCH v2 03/23] mm/vma: add further vma_flags_t unions ---

> diff --git a/mm/vma.h b/mm/vma.h
> +	union {
> +		/* Temporary while VMA flags are being converted. */
> +		vm_flags_t vm_flags;
> +		vma_flags_t vma_flags;
> +	};

Checked the layout: vm_flags_t is "unsigned long"; vma_flags_t is
{ unsigned long __vma_flags[1] } because NUM_VMA_FLAG_BITS ==
BITS_PER_LONG.  Both are the same size with the same bit layout, so
reads and writes through either union member are safe.  The same
reasoning applies to the mm_struct def_flags/def_vma_flags union in
mm_types.h.  Existing callers of def_flags such as check_brk_limits()
and brk() continue to work through the vm_flags_t member unchanged.

[ ... ]

--- [PATCH v2 05/23] mm/vma: use new VMA flags for sticky flags logic ---

> -#define VM_STICKY (VM_SOFTDIRTY | VM_MAYBE_GUARD)
> +#ifdef CONFIG_MEM_SOFT_DIRTY
> +#define VMA_STICKY_FLAGS mk_vma_flags(VMA_SOFTDIRTY_BIT, \
> +                                      VMA_MAYBE_GUARD_BIT)
> +#else
> +#define VMA_STICKY_FLAGS mk_vma_flags(VMA_MAYBE_GUARD_BIT)
> +#endif

Verified: without CONFIG_MEM_SOFT_DIRTY, VM_SOFTDIRTY is defined as 0
so the old VM_STICKY collapsed to VM_MAYBE_GUARD.  VMA_STICKY_FLAGS
in that configuration is mk_vma_flags(VMA_MAYBE_GUARD_BIT), the same
single bit.  The sticky-flag accumulation logic in
vma_merge_existing_range() and vma_expand() is semantically unchanged.

Checked vma_supports_mlock() (patch 18): the current code tests
  vma->vm_flags & (VM_SPECIAL | VM_DROPPABLE)
When VM_DROPPABLE is not configured it is VM_NONE (0), so that term
is always false.  vma_test_single_mask() is defined to return false
when the input mask is empty, preserving that behaviour exactly.

[ ... ]

--- [PATCH v2 17/23] mm: convert do_brk_flags() to use vma_flags_t ---

In the base tree, do_brk_flags() contains:

  vm_flags |= VM_DATA_DEFAULT_FLAGS | VM_ACCOUNT | mm->def_flags;
  vm_flags = ksm_vma_flags(mm, NULL, vm_flags);

ksm_vma_flags() is declared in ksm.h returning vm_flags_t.  The
cover letter notes include/linux/ksm.h has 10 lines changed and
mm/ksm.c has 43 lines changed in this series.  Could you confirm
whether ksm_vma_flags() is updated to return vma_flags_t here, or
whether patch 17 uses a vma_flags_to_legacy()/legacy_to_vma_flags()
conversion at the call site?  The diff stats suggest it is addressed,
but the truncated patch text makes it hard to verify the exact
boundary between the two approaches.

[ ... ]

Overall: the union aliasing, sticky-flags conversion, the
vma_test_single_mask() empty-mask semantics, and the new bitmap
helpers all look correct.  The only clear defect is the missing const
on vma_flags_empty().

patches.title: [PATCH v2] kernel/fork: validate exit_signal in kernel_clone()

review.summary: The patch adds a valid_signal() check in kernel_clone() to reject exit_signal values in the range 65-255 that clone() can pass via the CSIGNAL mask but that are not valid POSIX signals. The fix is correct but leaves a stale comment in the kernel_clone() header that now contradicts the new behavior.
review.review_inline:
Author: Deepanshu Kartikey <kartikey406@gmail.com>

kernel/fork: validate exit_signal in kernel_clone()

I verified valid_signal(0) returns true (0 <= _NSIG), so kernel_thread()
and user_mode_thread() callers that pass exit_signal=0 are unaffected.
I also confirmed that do_notify_parent() already has the guard
`if (valid_signal(sig) && sig)` before calling __send_signal_locked(),
and that copy_clone_args_from_user() for clone3() already has the same
valid_signal() check, so there is no double-validation concern.

One issue:

> diff --git a/kernel/fork.c b/kernel/fork.c
> --- a/kernel/fork.c
> +++ b/kernel/fork.c

[ ... ]

>  /*
>   *  Ok, this is the main fork-routine.
>   *
>   * It copies the process, and if successful kick-starts
>   * it and waits for it to finish using the VM if required.
>   *
>   * args->exit_signal is expected to be checked for sanity by the caller.
>   */
>  pid_t kernel_clone(struct kernel_clone_args *args)

This comment says exit_signal sanity checking is the caller's
responsibility, but the patch moves that responsibility into
kernel_clone() itself.  Without updating this comment, future callers
may read it and believe they need to validate exit_signal themselves
(or worse, that kernel_clone() will not enforce it).  The comment
should be updated to reflect that kernel_clone() now performs this
check centrally.

> +	if (!valid_signal(args->exit_signal))
> +		return -EINVAL;
>  	/*
>  	 * Determine whether and which event to report to ptracer.  When

Minor style nit: the surrounding code separates logical sections with a
blank line, but there is no blank line between the new guard and the
block comment that follows it.

series.title: mm/userfaultfd: re-validate vma in mfill_atomic() loop under CONFIG_PER_VMA_LOCK
series.author: Deepanshu Kartikey <kartikey406@gmail.com>

patches.title: [PATCH] mm/userfaultfd: re-validate vma in mfill_atomic() loop under CONFIG_PER_VMA_LOCK

review.summary: Under CONFIG_PER_VMA_LOCK, a concurrent UFFDIO_UNREGISTER can split the VMA held by mfill_atomic() mid-loop, shrinking vm_end so that dst_addr falls outside the VMA bounds. The patch detects this per-iteration and re-looks up the VMA when stale.
review.review_inline:
Deepanshu Kartikey <kartikey406@gmail.com>

mm/userfaultfd: re-validate vma in mfill_atomic() loop under CONFIG_PER_VMA_LOCK

Verified the fix by tracing mfill_get_vma(), mfill_put_vma(),
uffd_mfill_lock(), and validate_dst_vma().  After the re-lookup,
state.dst_start is set to state.dst_addr and state.len covers the
remaining range to the original end, so uffd_mfill_lock() receives
the correct [dst_addr, original_dst_end) range and validate_dst_vma()
enforces the same VMA-containment invariant as the initial lookup.
mfill_get_pmd() follows immediately and refreshes state->pmd, so
there is no stale PMD pointer.  Error paths from mfill_get_vma()
break the loop and return correctly via 'copied ? copied : err'.
The check is a no-op under !CONFIG_PER_VMA_LOCK because mmap_read_lock
prevents concurrent splits entirely.

One minor style issue:

> 	while (state.src_addr < src_start + len) {
> 		VM_WARN_ON_ONCE(state.dst_addr >= dst_start + len);
> +	       /*
> +		* Under CONFIG_PER_VMA_LOCK, a concurrent UFFDIO_UNREGISTER can

The opening '/*' is indented with one tab plus seven spaces instead
of two tabs, misaligning it from both the '*' continuation lines and
the 'if' statement it guards.  Should be '\t\t/*' to match the rest
of the file and avoid a checkpatch WARNING.

patches.title: [PATCH] Docs: mmap_prepare: don't italicize structs

review.summary: Removes backtick quoting around 'struct page' references in mmap_prepare.rst so kernel-doc automarkup renders them as bold monospace rather than italic in the generated HTML output.
review.review_inline:
Randy Dunlap <rdunlap@infradead.org>

Docs: mmap_prepare: don't italicize structs

Documentation-only change. Verified: no code is modified, only RST
formatting. The fix is correct -- in kernel-doc RST, backtick-quoted
`struct foo` produces italic HTML output, while plain "struct foo" text
triggers kernel-doc's automarkup to emit bold monospace instead.

> -* mmap_action_map_kernel_pages() - Maps a specified array of `struct page`
> +* mmap_action_map_kernel_pages() - Maps a specified array of struct page
>    pointers in the VMA from a specific offset.

All three occurrences are handled consistently.

Looks good to me.

series.title: selftests/mm: skip several tests when thp is not available
series.author: Chunyu Hu <chuhu@redhat.com>

patches.title: [PATCH v2 0/5] selftests/mm: skip several tests when thp is not available
[PATCH v2 1/5] selftests/mm/guard-regions: skip collapse test when thp not enabled
[PATCH v2 2/5] selftests/mm: soft-dirty: skip two tests when thp is not available
[PATCH v2 3/5] selftests/mm: move write_file helper to vm_util
[PATCH v2 4/5] selftests/mm: split_huge_page_test: skip the test when thp is not available
[PATCH v2 5/5] selftests/mm: transhuge_stress: skip the test when thp not available

review.summary: This series adds thp_is_enabled() guards to five selftest programs so they emit a proper TAP SKIP result instead of failing when THP is disabled at runtime (e.g., on realtime kernels). Patch 3 consolidates the duplicated write_file() helper from thp_settings.c and split_huge_page_test.c into vm_util.
review.review_inline:
Chunyu Hu <chuhu@redhat.com>

selftests/mm: skip several tests when thp is not available

Verified the series against the source tree. No regressions found.

> diff --git a/tools/testing/selftests/mm/thp_settings.c b/tools/testing/selftests/mm/thp_settings.c
> --- a/tools/testing/selftests/mm/thp_settings.c
> +++ b/tools/testing/selftests/mm/thp_settings.c
> @@ -6,6 +6,7 @@
>  #include <string.h>
>  #include <unistd.h>
> 
> +#include "vm_util.h"
>  #include "thp_settings.h"
> 
> [ ... ]
> 
> -int write_file(const char *path, const char *buf, size_t buflen)
> +unsigned int write_file(const char *path, const char *buf, size_t buflen)

Checked all callers: write_num() and thp_write_string() in thp_settings.c
both test the return via "if (!write_file(...))", and khugepaged.c discards
it entirely.  The int -> unsigned int change is safe for all of them.  The
new implementation calls ksft_exit_fail_msg() on error; vm_util.c already
includes kselftest.h so this compiles correctly.  write_num()'s own error
path becomes dead code after the change (write_file() now exits before it
can return 0), but that is harmless.

> diff --git a/tools/testing/selftests/mm/transhuge-stress.c b/tools/testing/selftests/mm/transhuge-stress.c
> @@ -37,6 +38,9 @@ int main(int argc, char **argv)
> 
>  	ksft_print_header();
> 
> +	if (!thp_is_enabled())
> +		ksft_exit_skip("Transparent Hugepages not available\n");
> +
>  	ram = sysconf(_SC_PHYS_PAGES);

The guard is placed after ksft_print_header() and before ksft_set_plan(1),
so ksft_exit_skip() produces the correct "1..0 # SKIP" TAP line without
leaving a dangling plan.  Same placement verified in split_huge_page_test,
where the check is correctly inserted before the read_pmd_pagesize() call
that was the original failure point.

The soft-dirty fix (patch 2) correctly emits two ksft_test_result_skip()
calls to match the two sub-tests counted in the 1..19 plan, fixing the
"Planned tests != run tests (19 != 18)" TAP error.

Reviewed-by: Usman Arif <uarif@meta.com>

series.title: Migrate on fault for device pages
series.author: mpenttil@redhat.com

patches.title: [PATCH v6 0/6] Migrate on fault for device pages
[PATCH v6 1/6] mm:/Kconfig changes for migrate on fault for device pages
[PATCH v6 2/6] mm: Add helper to convert HMM pfn to migrate pfn
[PATCH v6 3/6] mm/hmm: do the plumbing for HMM to participate in migration
[PATCH v6 4/6] mm: setup device page migration in HMM pagewalk
[PATCH v6 5/6] mm: add new testcase for the migrate on fault case
[PATCH v6 6/6] mm:/migrate_device.c: remove migrate_vma_collect_*()

review.summary: This series adds HMM_PFN_REQ_MIGRATE and associated plumbing so that hmm_range_fault() can install migration entries during the page walk, eliminating a separate migrate_vma_setup() pass. Three concrete bugs were found: a compile error in the !CONFIG_MIGRATION stub placement, a zero-npages bug in the test driver that silently skips all migration finalization leaving PTEs stuck as migration entries, and a potential NULL dereference in hmm_select_migrate().
review.review_inline:
From: Mika Penttilä <mpenttil@redhat.com>
Subject: Re: [PATCH v6 0/6] Migrate on fault for device pages

Hi Mika,

Thanks for the v6.  I found three issues below that need addressing.

--- [PATCH v6 3/6] mm/hmm: do the plumbing for HMM to participate in migration ---

> diff --git a/include/linux/migrate.h b/include/linux/migrate.h
> @@ -104,6 +104,15 @@ static inline void
>  softleaf_entry_wait_on_locked(softleaf_t entry, spinlock_t *p)
>  	WARN_ON_ONCE(1);
>
>  	spin_unlock(ptl);
> +
> +enum migrate_vma_info {
> +	MIGRATE_VMA_SELECT_NONE = 0,
> +	MIGRATE_VMA_SELECT_COMPOUND = MIGRATE_VMA_SELECT_NONE,
> +};
> +
> +static inline enum migrate_vma_info hmm_select_migrate(struct hmm_range *range)
> +{
> +	return MIGRATE_VMA_SELECT_NONE;
>  }
>
>  #endif /* CONFIG_MIGRATION */

The hunk context confirms these additions land *inside* the body of
softleaf_entry_wait_on_locked(), between spin_unlock(ptl) and its
closing brace.  Defining a static inline function inside another
function body is not valid C (GCC nested-function syntax is not
used here), and the closing brace that was softleaf_entry_wait_on_locked's
own brace now becomes hmm_select_migrate's closing brace, leaving
softleaf_entry_wait_on_locked itself unclosed.  Any config that enables
HMM_MIRROR without CONFIG_MIGRATION will hit a compile error;
HMM_MIRROR depends only on MMU, so that combination is reachable
independently of the DEVICE_MIGRATION select added in patch 1.

The stub needs to appear after the closing brace of
softleaf_entry_wait_on_locked, not before it.

--- [PATCH v6 4/6] mm: setup device page migration in HMM pagewalk ---

> diff --git a/include/linux/migrate.h b/include/linux/migrate.h
> @@ -200,10 +201,14 @@ struct migrate_vma {
>  static inline enum migrate_vma_info hmm_select_migrate(struct hmm_range *range)
>  {
> -	return 0;
> +	enum migrate_vma_info minfo;
> +
> +	minfo = (range->default_flags & HMM_PFN_REQ_MIGRATE) ?
> +		range->migrate->flags : 0;
> +
> +	return minfo;
>  }

If HMM_PFN_REQ_MIGRATE is set in default_flags but range->migrate is
NULL, this dereferences a NULL pointer.  HMM_PFN_REQ_MIGRATE and
range->migrate are a coupled pair with no API enforcement between
them.  hmm_select_migrate() is called from hmm_pfns_fill(),
hmm_vma_handle_migrate_prepare_pmd(), and hmm_vma_handle_migrate_prepare(),
so a misconfigured caller would crash the kernel in any of those paths.
Should the condition also check range->migrate before dereferencing it?

--- [PATCH v6 5/6] mm: add new testcase for the migrate on fault case ---

> +static int dmirror_fault_and_migrate_to_device(struct dmirror *dmirror,
> +					       struct hmm_dmirror_cmd *cmd)
> +{
> +	...
> +	struct migrate_vma migrate = { 0 };
> +	struct hmm_range range = { 0 };
> +	...
> +	migrate.pgmap_owner = dmirror->mdevice;
> +	migrate.src = src_pfns;
> +	migrate.dst = dst_pfns;
> +	migrate.flags = MIGRATE_VMA_SELECT_SYSTEM;

Neither migrate.start nor migrate.end is ever written.  Both remain 0.

> +	for (next = start; next < end; next = range.end) {
> +		range.start = next;
> +		range.end = min(end, next + (PFNS_ARRAY_SIZE << PAGE_SHIFT));
> +		...
> +		ret = do_fault_and_migrate(dmirror, &range);

Inside do_fault_and_migrate(), migrate_hmm_range_setup() computes:

    migrate->npages = (migrate->end - migrate->start) >> PAGE_SHIFT;

With start=0 and end=0 this is always 0.  The for-loop in
migrate_hmm_range_setup() never runs, migrate->cpages stays 0, and
then migrate_vma_pages() and migrate_vma_finalize() both iterate over
0 entries in __migrate_device_pages() / __migrate_device_finalize()
and do nothing.

This means any migration entries installed inside hmm_range_fault()
are never removed.  Subsequent accesses to those virtual addresses
will block on migration_entry_wait() indefinitely.

Compare with dmirror_migrate_to_device(), which sets args.start and
args.end explicitly on every loop iteration before calling
migrate_vma_setup().  The new path skips that.

Either migrate_hmm_range_setup() should derive npages from
(range->end - range->start) >> PAGE_SHIFT rather than from
migrate->end - migrate->start, or the caller must set migrate.start
and migrate.end inside the loop.

Also worth noting: do_fault_and_migrate() calls all four migration
functions regardless of what dmirror_range_fault() returned.  If the
range is invalidated and -EBUSY comes back with migration entries only
partially installed, the cleanup chain still needs to run.

Regards

series.title: [LSF/MM/BPF TOPIC]  mm/mempolicy: introduce socket-aware weighted interleave
series.author: Rakie Kim <rakie.kim@sk.com>

patches.title: [LSF/MM/BPF TOPIC] [RFC PATCH 0/4] mm/mempolicy: introduce socket-aware weighted interleave
[RFC PATCH 1/4] mm/numa: introduce nearest_nodes_nodemask()
[RFC PATCH 2/4] mm/memory-tiers: introduce socket-aware topology management for NUMA nodes
[RFC PATCH 3/4] mm/memory-tiers: register CXL nodes to socket-aware packages via initiator
[RFC PATCH 4/4] mm/mempolicy: enhance weighted interleave with socket-aware locality

review.summary: This four-patch RFC introduces socket-aware weighted interleave by adding a NUMA helper to find all nearest nodes, a memory-package topology layer grouping NUMA nodes by physical socket, CXL driver integration to bind CXL nodes to their initiator's package, and mempolicy changes that filter weighted-interleave candidates to the current socket before applying weights. Several correctness issues in the stubs and the fallback path of policy_resolve_package_nodes() need fixing before this is ready.

series.title: userfaultfd: unassigned vma leads to a potential unreleased locks
series.author: Edward Adam Davis <eadavis@qq.com>

patches.title: [PATCH next] userfaultfd: unassigned vma leads to a potential unreleased locks

review.summary: The patch fixes a lock leak in mfill_get_vma() where mmap_lock and map_changing_lock were not released on error paths because state->vma was only assigned at the out: label, causing mfill_put_vma() to return early (seeing NULL) and skip both unlocks.
review.review_inline:
From: Edward Adam Davis <eadavis@qq.com>
Subject: [PATCH next] userfaultfd: unassigned vma leads to a potential unreleased locks

Verified by reading mm/userfaultfd.c in full. The analysis below
confirms the fix is correct.

> diff --git a/mm/userfaultfd.c b/mm/userfaultfd.c
> @@ -218,6 +218,7 @@ static int mfill_get_vma(struct mfill_state *state)
>  	if (IS_ERR(dst_vma))
>  		return PTR_ERR(dst_vma);
>  
> +	state->vma = dst_vma;
>  	/*
>  	 * If memory mappings are changing because of non-cooperative
>  	 * operation (e.g. mremap) running in parallel, bail out and
> @@ -257,7 +258,6 @@ static int mfill_get_vma(struct mfill_state *state)
>  		goto out_unlock;
>  
>  out:
> -	state->vma = dst_vma;
>  	return 0;
>  
>  out_unlock:
>  	mfill_put_vma(state);

The bug: mfill_put_vma() uses state->vma as a sentinel to decide
whether to release locks:

    static void mfill_put_vma(struct mfill_state *state)
    {
        if (!state->vma)
            return;           /* skips both unlocks */
        up_read(&state->ctx->map_changing_lock);
        uffd_mfill_unlock(state->vma);
        ...
    }

Before this patch, every goto out_unlock after down_read() --
the mmap_changing check, the WARN_ON_ONCE, the WP flag check,
and the ops checks -- called mfill_put_vma() with state->vma still
NULL, silently leaking both mmap_lock and map_changing_lock.

The fix is correct. There is no code path between the new
state->vma assignment and the subsequent down_read() that can
reach out_unlock, so the invariant holds: whenever state->vma is
non-NULL inside mfill_get_vma, both locks are held and
mfill_put_vma() will release them symmetrically.

Reviewed-by: [reviewer]

patches.title: [PATCH] zram: propagate read_from_bdev_async() errors

review.summary: The patch changes read_from_bdev_async() from void to int, returning -ENOMEM on kmalloc/bio_alloc failures and propagating that error through read_from_bdev() so that zram_bio_read() can set BLK_STS_IOERR on the parent bio instead of silently swallowing the error.
review.review_inline:
Sergey Senozhatsky <senozhatsky@chromium.org>

zram: propagate read_from_bdev_async() errors

Verified the full call chain and bio reference counting. The change
looks correct.

> -static void read_from_bdev_async(struct zram *zram, struct page *page,
> -				 u32 index, unsigned long blk_idx,
> -				 struct bio *parent)
> +static int read_from_bdev_async(struct zram *zram, struct page *page,
> +				u32 index, unsigned long blk_idx,
> +				struct bio *parent)

Checked bio_inc_remaining() accounting: it is only reached after both
kmalloc_obj() and bio_alloc() succeed, so on the two new -ENOMEM
returns the parent bio's remaining count is never incremented. The
parent bio is therefore still completed exactly once by bio_endio(bio)
at the end of zram_bio_read(), with bi_status set to BLK_STS_IOERR by
the caller's error check.

> -	read_from_bdev_async(zram, page, index, blk_idx, parent);
> -	return 0;
> +	return read_from_bdev_async(zram, page, index, blk_idx, parent);

read_from_bdev_async() has exactly one call site. The return value of
read_from_bdev() is checked for < 0 by every caller: zram_bio_read()
and zram_bvec_write_partial() (via zram_read_page()). Error
propagation is complete and consistent with the existing sync path.

series.title: mm/damon: let DAMON be paused and resumed
series.author: SeongJae Park <sj@kernel.org>

patches.title: [RFC PATCH 00/10] mm/damon: let DAMON be paused and resumed
[RFC PATCH 01/10] mm/damon/core: introduce damon_ctx->paused
[RFC PATCH 02/10] mm/damon/sysfs: add pause file under context dir
[RFC PATCH 03/10] Docs/mm/damon/design: update for context pause/resume feature
[RFC PATCH 04/10] Docs/admin-guide/mm/damon/usage: update for pause file
[RFC PATCH 05/10] Docs/ABI/damon: update for pause sysfs file
[RFC PATCH 06/10] mm/damon/tests/core-kunit: test pause commitment
[RFC PATCH 07/10] selftests/damon/_damon_sysfs: support pause file staging
[RFC PATCH 08/10] selftests/damon/drgn_dump_damon_status: dump pause
[RFC PATCH 09/10] selftests/damon/sysfs.py: check pause on assert_ctx_committed()
[RFC PATCH 10/10] selftets/damon/sysfs.py: pause DAMON before dumping status

review.summary: The series introduces a damon_ctx->pause flag so kdamond can be paused and resumed without losing self-trained state, and exposes it through a new sysfs 'pause' file under the context directory. The core mechanism is correct, but the sysfs context allocator leaves the new pause field uninitialized.
review.review_inline:
SeongJae Park <sj@kernel.org>

mm/damon: let DAMON be paused and resumed

Verified: error paths in damon_commit_ctx, locking (pause only written
from kdamond thread via damon_call callback, no race), stop semantics
(kdamond_need_stop in the pause inner loop handles kthread_stop
correctly), and counter behaviour (passed_sample_intervals not
advanced while paused).

One issue found:

> diff --git a/mm/damon/sysfs.c b/mm/damon/sysfs.c
> --- a/mm/damon/sysfs.c
> +++ b/mm/damon/sysfs.c

[ ... ]

> +	bool pause;
>  };
>  
>  static struct damon_sysfs_context *damon_sysfs_context_alloc(
>  		enum damon_ops_id ops_id)
>  {
>  	struct damon_sysfs_context *context = kmalloc_obj(*context);
>  	if (!context)
>  		return NULL;
>  	context->kobj = (struct kobject){};
>  	context->ops_id = ops_id;
>  	context->addr_unit = 1;
>  	return context;
>  }

damon_sysfs_context_alloc() uses kmalloc_obj(), which maps to kmalloc()
and does not zero the allocation.  The new pause field is not explicitly
initialised here, unlike ops_id and addr_unit.  Two concrete problems
follow:

1. pause_show() returns a garbage 'Y'/'N' until the user first writes
   to the file.

2. damon_sysfs_apply_inputs() copies sys_ctx->pause into the live
   damon_ctx before damon_start() is called:

>  +	ctx->pause = sys_ctx->pause;

   If the user never writes to the pause sysfs file, ctx->pause
   receives an uninitialised byte and DAMON may start in an
   unexpectedly paused state.

Should damon_sysfs_context_alloc() set context->pause = false
explicitly, the same way it sets context->addr_unit = 1?

[ ... ]

> +		while (ctx->pause) {
> +			if (kdamond_need_stop(ctx))
> +				goto done;
> +			kdamond_usleep(ctx->attrs.sample_interval);
> +			/* allow caller unset pause via damon_call() */
> +			kdamond_call(ctx, false);
> +		}

The rest of the core change looks correct.  passed_sample_intervals is
not incremented in the pause loop, so aggregation and interval-tuning
counters do not advance while paused.  The kdamond_need_stop() check
ensures kthread_stop() is honoured without blocking more than one
sample_interval.  Since ctx->pause is only ever written by the kdamond
thread (via damon_call callbacks executing damon_commit_ctx), there is
no concurrent-access issue with the reads in the pause loop.

series.title: mm/damon/stat: monitor all System RAM resources
series.author: SeongJae Park <sj@kernel.org>

patches.title: [PATCH v2] mm/damon/stat: monitor all System RAM resources

review.summary: The patch fixes DAMON_STAT to monitor all System RAM resources by spanning from the lowest System RAM start to the highest System RAM end, instead of only the single biggest System RAM region. This matches the documented behavior on NUMA systems with multiple System RAM resources.
review.review_inline:
SeongJae Park <sj@kernel.org>

mm/damon/stat: monitor all System RAM resources

Verified the following before reviewing:
 - walk_system_ram_res() via walk_res_desc() advances start = res.end + 1
   after each callback, so resources are visited in ascending address
   order.
 - damon_stat_system_ram_walk_fn() correctly latches res.start on the
   first (lowest) resource and always overwrites res.end, so the
   accumulated range spans [lowest_RAM_start, highest_RAM_end].
 - The !arg.walked guard after walk_system_ram_res() maps cleanly to the
   old res.end < res.start guard in damon_find_biggest_system_ram().
 - damon_stat_res_to_core_addr() is an exact copy of
   damon_res_to_core_addr() in core.c, including the div_u64() guard.
 - The res.end + 1 exclusive-end conversion matches the old code path in
   damon_find_biggest_system_ram().

> +static int damon_stat_set_monitoring_region(struct damon_target *t,
> +		unsigned long addr_unit, unsigned long min_region_sz)
> +{
> +	struct damon_addr_range addr_range;
> +	struct damon_stat_system_ram_range_walk_arg arg = {};
> +
> +	walk_system_ram_res(0, -1, &arg, damon_stat_system_ram_walk_fn);
> +	if (!arg.walked)
> +		return -EINVAL;
> +	addr_range.start = damon_stat_res_to_core_addr(
> +			arg.res.start, addr_unit);
> +	addr_range.end = damon_stat_res_to_core_addr(
> +			arg.res.end + 1, addr_unit);
> +	return damon_set_regions(t, &addr_range, 1, min_region_sz);
> +}

Passing 1 as nr_ranges to damon_set_regions() is correct for the single
consolidated range. The v2 changelog notes this fixed a wrong argument
(addr_unit passed instead of 1 in v1).

Looks good to me.

series.title: mm/mglru: fix cgroup OOM during MGLRU state switching
series.author: Leno Hou via B4 Relay <devnull+lenohou.gmail.com@kernel.org>

patches.title: [PATCH v3 0/2] mm/mglru: fix cgroup OOM during MGLRU state switching
[PATCH v3 1/2] mm/mglru: fix cgroup OOM during MGLRU state switching
[PATCH v3 2/2] mm/mglru: maintain workingset refault context across state transitions

review.summary: Two-patch series introducing a 'draining' intermediate state (lru_drain_core static key) during MGLRU enable/disable transitions to prevent a reclaim vacuum causing cgroup OOM, plus shadow entry context tracking to dispatch workingset refaults to the correct handler after state changes.
review.review_inline:
From: reviewer

mm/mglru: fix cgroup OOM during MGLRU state switching
mm/mglru: maintain workingset refault context across state transitions

The problem being solved is real: lru_gen_change_state() flips the
global static branch before draining/filling per-lruvec lists, leaving
a window where the reclaimer sees one state but pages still live in the
other set of lists.

> diff --git a/include/linux/mm_inline.h b/include/linux/mm_inline.h
> +static inline bool lru_gen_draining(void)
> +{
> +	DECLARE_STATIC_KEY_FALSE(lru_drain_core);
> +
> +	return static_branch_unlikely(&lru_drain_core);
> +}

The DECLARE_STATIC_KEY_FALSE here is an extern reference.  I grepped
the entire tree and found no DEFINE_STATIC_KEY_FALSE(lru_drain_core)
in any compiled .c file.  Without a definition this is a link error.
Is the DEFINE in the truncated portion of the mm/vmscan.c hunk?  The
patch as posted cannot be built as-is; please repost with the complete
diff.

> diff --git a/mm/rmap.c b/mm/rmap.c
> -		if (lru_gen_enabled() && pvmw.pte) {
> +		if (lru_gen_enabled() && !lru_gen_draining() && pvmw.pte) {

When disabling MGLRU, lru_gen_enabled() is already flipped to false
before draining begins (static_branch_disable_cpuslocked runs first in
lru_gen_change_state()), so the outer lru_gen_enabled() check already
blocks lru_gen_look_around() in that direction.  The
!lru_gen_draining() guard is only effective during the enable direction
(false -> filling).  Is skipping look_around() during fill intentional,
and is there a concrete hazard it prevents?

> diff --git a/mm/swap.c b/mm/swap.c
> -	if (lru_gen_enabled()) {
> +	if (folio_lru_gen(folio) != -1) {
>  		lru_gen_inc_refs(folio);
>  		return;
>  	}

folio_mark_accessed() runs without any lock.  folio_lru_gen() reads
folio->flags with READ_ONCE() while drain_evictable() holds lruvec_lock
and clears LRU_GEN_MASK via set_mask_bits().  Since lru_gen_inc_refs()
uses set_mask_bits() on LRU_REFS_MASK (a disjoint bit range in the same
word), the cmpxchg loop handles concurrent writers correctly -- this is
safe.

However, there is a window where folio_lru_gen() returns -1 not because
the folio is on a traditional LRU list but because it has been isolated
from an MGLRU list (lru_gen_del_folio() clears LRU_GEN_MASK during
isolation) and not yet putback.  In that narrow window the traditional
folio_mark_accessed() path runs for a folio that originated in MGLRU.
Is this double-counting or mis-accounting a concern, or is it harmless
because the folio will be put back on the right list afterwards?

> -	bool active = folio_test_active(folio) || lru_gen_enabled();
> +	bool active = folio_test_active(folio) || (folio_lru_gen(folio) != -1);

lru_deactivate_file() is called under lruvec_lock (via lru_deactivate_
fn / folio_batch_move_lru), so folio_lru_gen() is stable here.
The substitution is correct.

> diff --git a/mm/vmscan.c b/mm/vmscan.c
> +	/* whether in lru gen scan context */
> +	unsigned int lru_gen:1;

> +	sc->lru_gen = 0;
>  	nr_reclaimed = shrink_folio_list(&folio_list, pgdat, sc, &stat,
> 					 false, lruvec_memcg(lruvec));

sc is passed by pointer down the call stack; shrink_inactive_list()
writes sc->lru_gen = 0 and evict_folios() (in the truncated hunk)
writes sc->lru_gen = 1.  For the joint-reclaim draining path the
ordering must be exact: MGLRU path sets 1, traditional path resets 0.
Consider whether lru_gen should be passed as an explicit local bool
to __remove_mapping() instead of living in scan_control, to make the
ownership clearer and prevent future callers from getting the ordering
wrong.

> -	} else if (!mapping || !__remove_mapping(mapping, folio, true,
> +	} else if (!mapping || !__remove_mapping(mapping, folio, true,
>  						 sc->target_mem_cgroup, sc))

> +long remove_mapping(struct address_space *mapping, struct folio *folio)
> +{
> +	if (__remove_mapping(mapping, folio, false, NULL, NULL)) {

remove_mapping() passes NULL for sc.  sc->lru_gen is only accessed
inside the 'if (reclaimed && ...)' blocks in __remove_mapping(), and
reclaimed=false here, so sc is never dereferenced.  Safe, but the NULL
contract is implicit.  A comment noting that sc may be NULL only when
reclaimed=false would help future readers.

> diff --git a/mm/vmscan.c b/mm/vmscan.c (patch 2)
> +	.lru_gen = lru_gen_enabled(),

In reclaim_clean_pages_from_list() and reclaim_folio_list(), folios
are already isolated so folio_lru_gen() returns -1 for all of them.
Using lru_gen_enabled() as a global snapshot is therefore the best
available heuristic, but during a state transition it can encode the
wrong context into shadow entries (e.g., MGLRU just disabled, pages
already isolated before the flag flipped).  Is this an acceptable
approximation given the retry-based mitigation described in the cover
letter?

[ ... ]

The mm/workingset.c changes for patch 2 are not visible in the
posted diff.  Please clarify:

- Where is WORKINGSET_MGLRU_SHIFT defined, and how does adding one
  bit to the shadow entry affect EVICTION_SHIFT and the available
  timestamp range?  The existing BUILD_BUG_ON in lru_gen_eviction()
  guards against LRU_GEN_WIDTH + LRU_REFS_WIDTH overflow, but not
  against the timestamp precision loss caused by raising EVICTION_SHIFT.

- How does workingset_eviction() choose between lru_gen_eviction() and
  the traditional pack_shadow() path based on the new bool lru_gen
  parameter?  The two functions produce incompatible shadow encodings;
  the is_mglru bit must be detectable before any other field is
  unpacked.

- How does workingset_refault()/workingset_test_recent() extract and
  dispatch on that bit when the current lru_gen_enabled() state no
  longer matches the eviction-time state?

The truncated vmscan.c hunk also hides the definition of lru_drain_core,
the lru_gen_change_state() modifications that arm/disarm the draining
state, and the shrink_lruvec() joint-reclaim path -- all of which are
the core of patch 1.  Please repost with the full diff.

series.title: Docs/mm/damon: document exclusivity of special-purpose modules
series.author: SeongJae Park <sj@kernel.org>

patches.title: [PATCH v2] Docs/mm/damon: document exclusivity of special-purpose modules

review.summary: Documentation-only patch that adds a cross-reference label and note to design.rst documenting the exclusive-run behaviour of DAMON special-purpose modules, then links to it from lru_sort.rst, reclaim.rst, and stat.rst.
review.review_inline:
From: Liew Rui Yan <aethernet65535@gmail.com>

Docs/mm/damon: document exclusivity of special-purpose modules

Verified against the source code.  All three modules call
damon_start() with exclusive=true:

  reclaim.c:  err = damon_start(&ctx, 1, true);
  lru_sort.c: err = damon_start(&ctx, 1, true);
  stat.c:     err = damon_start(&damon_stat_context, 1, true);

And core.c enforces the constraint under damon_lock:

  if ((exclusive && nr_running_ctxs) ||
          (!exclusive && running_exclusive_ctxs)) {
      mutex_unlock(&damon_lock);
      return -EBUSY;
  }

The documented -EBUSY return value and the exclusive-run wording are
both accurate.

> diff --git a/Documentation/mm/damon/design.rst b/Documentation/mm/damon/design.rst
> +.. _damon_design_special_purpose_modules_exclusivity:
> +
> +Note that these modules currently run in an exclusive manner.  If one
> +of those is already running, others will return ``-EBUSY`` upon start
> +requests.

The RST label and the cross-references in the three admin-guide files
are syntactically correct.  A label placed before a plain paragraph
(rather than a section heading) is valid Sphinx RST and matches
patterns used elsewhere in the kernel docs.

Looks good to me.
